title: Case on Impeachment of Prosecutor
text:
- 185 -Case on Impeachment of Prosecutor
[2023Hun-Na2, May 30, 2024]
This case involves the National Assembly filing a petition for the 
impeachment of Respondent on the grounds that Respondent’s institution 
of a prosecution violated the law. The Court rejected the petition for 
impeachment. 
Background of the Case
On March 29, 2010, a prosecutor from the Seoul Eastern District 
Prosecutors’ Office deferred the prosecution of Y.W. regarding an 
alleged violation of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act (hereinafter 
referred to as the “Previous Deferred Prosecution”). This charge 
concerned Y.W.’s conduct of engaging in foreign exchange business by 
acting as a foreign exchange agent for domestic and international 
remittances without registration under the Foreign Exchange Transactions 
Act.
On February 26, 2013, a prosecutor from the Seoul Central District 
Prosecutors’ Office charged and detained Y.W. for alleged violations, 
including a National Security Act violation (espionage). (Y.W.’s earlier 
charge of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act violation was not 
included in the new criminal counts.) The trial court acquitted Y.W. of 
all charges relating to the National Security Act violations and convicted 
him on the other charges, including a violation of the North Korean 
Defectors Protection and Settlement Support Act. He was sentenced to 
one-year imprisonment with labor, with a two-year suspension of the 
sentence. During the appeal, National Intelligence Service employees 
forged, inter alia, Y.W.’s records of entry and exit to and from North 
Korea and submitted them as evidence through the prosecutors involved 
in the trial. In February 2014, it was revealed through a response from 
Chinese authorities that they were forged. On April 25, 2014, the 
- 186 -appellate court acquitted Y.W. of all charges relating to the National 
Security Act violations, but convicted him on the other charges, 
maintaining the sentence of the trial court.
On March 21, 2014, a complaint was submitted to the Seoul Central 
District Prosecutors’ Office regarding, inter alia, Y.W.’s alleged 
violation of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act. Respondent, who 
was assigned to the case as a prosecutor of this office, reinvestigated the 
allegation and, on May 9, 2014, charged Y.W. with a violation of the 
Foreign Exchange Transactions Act and obstruction of official duties by 
fraudulent means (hereinafter referred to as the “Instant Prosecution” for 
Respondent’s prosecution of the Foreign Exchange Transactions charge).
The trial court convicted Y.W. on all charges and imposed a fine of 
10 million won. The appellate court determined that the Instant 
Prosecution constituted an abuse of prosecutorial discretion. It rejected 
the charge relating to the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act violation 
and convicted Y.W. only of obstruction of official duties by fraudulent 
means, sentencing him to a fine of 7 million won. The prosecutor 
appealed this judgment, but the Supreme Court rejected the appeal, 
stating, inter alia, that there was no misunderstanding of the legal 
principles concerning the abuse of prosecutorial discretion in the lower 
court’s ruling. 
On September 22, 2023, the National Assembly filed a petition for the 
impeachment of Respondent.
Summary of the Decision
1. Rejection opinion of three Justices
Respondent determined that a reinvestigation into Y.W.’s alleged 
violation of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act was necessary and 
initiated the investigation. It was revealed that Y.W. actively participated 
- 187 -in the violation of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act in collusion 
with his maternal uncle. Further, despite being a Chinese national living 
abroad, he concealed this fact and was recognized as a North Korean 
defector, committing various crimes afterward. Respondent instituted the 
Instant Prosecution based on these circumstances, which were sufficient 
to overturn the Previous Deferred Prosecution and charge Y.W. 
Therefore, the Instant Prosecution cannot be considered a violation of the 
following: Article 123 of the Criminal Act, which states, “A public 
official who, by abusing his or her official authority, causes a person to 
perform conduct that is not to be performed by the person, or obstructs 
the person from exercising a right that the person is entitled to exercise, 
shall be punished by imprisonment with labor for not more than five 
years, suspension of qualifications for not more than ten years, or fine 
not exceeding ten million won”; Article 4, Section (2) of the former 
Public Prosecutors’ Office Act, which provides, “In performing his or 
her duties, each prosecutor as a civil servant of all citizens shall 
maintain political neutrality and shall not abuse any authority vested in 
him or her”; and Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act, which 
prescribes, “Every public official shall observe statutes or regulations and 
faithfully perform his or her duties.”
2. Rejection opinion of two Justices
Compared to the Previous Deferred Prosecution, the total transaction 
amount for the offense of violating the Foreign Exchange Transactions 
Act has actually decreased, and the key criminal activity remains the 
same. Given, inter alia, these facts, it is difficult to conclude that 
Respondent’s investigation revealed circumstances warranting the 
overturning of the Previous Deferred Prosecution and the charging of 
Y.W. Therefore, the Instant Prosecution is a violation of Article 4, 
Section (2) of the former Public Prosecutors’ Office Act and Article 56 
of the State Public Officials Act. However, it is difficult to consider that 
the degree of unlawfulness and unfairness in the Instant Prosecution has 
- 188 -reached a level where it can be evaluated as a performance of duties 
that fundamentally deviates from their original purpose, and thus as 
constituting abuse of authority under Article 123 of the Criminal Act. 
Moreover, we do not find that Respondent intended to abuse his 
authority, so it cannot be concluded that the Instant Prosecution violated 
Article 123 of the Criminal Act. 
Considering, inter alia, that it is difficult to view Respondent as 
having violated the law with the active intention of undermining the 
legal order; that Respondent did not seek to exercise the state’s punitive 
power in contradiction to substantive truth; that there are court 
judgments identifying the Instant Prosecution as an abuse of 
prosecutorial authority, which make it unlikely that such abuse will 
recur; and that Respondent has served in public office for over nine 
years since the Instant Prosecution, mitigating its negative impact on the 
constitutional order, we find it difficult to conclude that there is a 
ground justifying the removal of Respondent from office.
Summary of Dissenting Opinion of Four Justices 
Respondent initiated a full-scale investigation into Y.W.’s alleged 
violation of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act without adequate 
review, despite no reasons for reinvestigation. He proceeded with the 
Instant Prosecution even though there were no circumstances justifying 
the overturning of the Previous Deferred Prosecution and the charging of 
Y.W. This action was driven by an intention to impose substantial 
disadvantages on Y.W., who was found not guilty of charges including 
a National Security Act violation (espionage) after it was revealed that 
the evidence presented by the prosecution was fabricated. Consequently, 
the Instant Prosecution constitutes a violation of Article 4, Section (2) of 
the former Public Prosecutors’ Office Act, Article 56 of the State Public 
Officials Act, and Article 123 of the Criminal Act.
- 189 -The Instant Prosecution was conducted by a prosecutor who, despite 
being a representative of the public interest and a defender of human 
rights, misused his authority to undermine the fairness of the prosecution 
and infringe the legitimate interests of the suspect, rendering the degree 
of legal violation extremely serious. The benefits of upholding the 
Constitution by removing Respondent from office far outweigh the 
national losses associated with such removal. Therefore, we find 
sufficient grounds to justify the removal of Respondent from office.
